plan.contextChunk:
\end{bmatrix}
W_Y 
\end{aligned}$$ 

In this case, the focal length $f$ will have a strong
effect on the appearance of the motion field. For very large $f$, we can
approximate the 2D motion field by $\dot{x} \approx f W_Y$ and
$\dot{y} \approx 0$. The resulting motion field is approximately
constant across the entire image and looks like lateral translation
motion. For very small $f$, the motion field will be similar to the one
produced by a homography and it will be very different to a lateral
camera motion. The flows shown in @fig-motion_wy_f03_f1_f3 correspond
to $f=1/3$, $f=1$, and $f=3$.

![Motion flows corresponding to a rotation around the 
$Y$-axis. (left) $f = 1/3$. (middle) $f = 1$. (right) $f = 3$.](figures/optical_flow/camera_motion_field_rotation_three_f.png){width="100%" #fig-motion_wy_f03_f1_f3}

Camera rotation around the $Y$-axis does not inform about the scene
structure, but it informs about the camera parameters. The magnitude of
$W_Y$ only affects the scaling of the motion vectors, but it does not
change their orientation.

In the case of camera rotation, for a large angle (or a large
$\Delta t$), the relationship between the image at time $t$ and the
image at time $t+\Delta t$ is a homography.

### Motion under Varying Focal Length

Before moving into motion estimation, let's consider one final scenario:
a static camera observing a static scene, but the focal length changes
over time. What will the motion field be? In this setting, despite that

elements: three rectangular image planes side by side
grid of 2D arrows representing motion vectors on each plane
arrows varying in magnitude and direction based on focal length
bounding boxes around each plane
labels under each plane: 'f = 1/3', 'f = 1', 'f = 3'

interactions.label: Focal length (f)
interactions.teaches: Changing the focal length alters the appearance of the motion field in the image, showing how optical flow depends on camera intrinsics.

interactions.label: Y-rotation rate (W_Y)
interactions.teaches: Adjusting the rotation rate scales the size of all vectors, demonstrating that motion field magnitude depends on angular velocity but the pattern shape is fixed.

demo_steps.title: Low focal length, complex flow
demo_steps.narration: Let's start with a small focal length, f = 1/3. Notice how the motion vectors radiate outward, showing an exaggerated and nonlinear effect due to camera rotation. The flow pattern is very different from simple lateral translation.

demo_steps.title: Moderate focal length, mid transition
demo_steps.narration: Now set f = 1. The vectors become more horizontal and less divergent, indicating that the image motion is transitioning from homography-like to translation-like as the focal length grows.

demo_steps.title: Large focal length, pure translation
demo_steps.narration: At a large focal length, f = 3, all the vectors are nearly constant in the horizontal direction. This mimics the optical flow of pure sideways camera translation, as the effect of rotation becomes nearly uniform across the image.

demo_steps.title: Increase rotation speed
demo_steps.narration: If we increase the Y-rotation rate, you’ll see that all motion vectors grow in length, but their directions and the overall flow pattern stay the same. Only the speed of apparent movement changes, not the structure.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Orthographic top-down view framing all three planes side by side, with minor zoom so all motion fields are visible and easily compared.
interactionPlan.notes: For clarity, keep all vector arrows within each image plane, and synchronize the grid layout so users can easily visualize changes as f or rotation are varied. Use consistent arrow scaling for fair magnitude comparison across different parameter values.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: Panels are too small and vertically squished compared to the source figure.
Arrow density and size do not match; vectors in the render are less dense and much shorter, especially at the periphery.
No arrows extend outside the rectangle borders as in the leftmost source panel.
Panel and arrow spacing is not as wide as in the source (boxes are closer together).
UI controls and buttons are visible in the HTML output but are not part of the original figure.
evaluation.notes: Core panel structure and arrows are present and recognizable, but panel scaling, arrow density/length, and boundary details diverge from the source.
action_items: Increase panel size and aspect ratio to better match the reference proportions.
Increase arrow density and extend arrows beyond the box boundaries in the left panel, matching original vector field extents.
Loosen panel spacing and decrease button/control prominence during export for better figure matching.
Tune arrow scaling and directions to closely mimic vector lengths and curvatures shown in the original.
Adjust label placement for closer vertical alignment with the original.

⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: Extra sliders and buttons are present in the generated figure that are not in the original.
Placement of UI obstructs the leftmost panel arrows.
Arrow density/distribution appears slightly sparser than in the reference for some rows/columns.
Small difference in arrowhead shape and stroke weight (rendered arrows are more filled 3D meshes rather than simple lines).
All panel labels and box proportions are accurate but the extra UI and caption offset the composition.
evaluation.notes: Panels, arrows, and labels are clear and models the motion field for f variations, but extra interactive UI distracts and slightly shifts layout.
action_items: Move the UI panel so it does not overlap the arrows or the left panel's frame.
Reduce arrow thickness and simplify arrowheads to better match source vector style.
Densify arrow grid if possible to closer match the arrow count/distribution in the original.
Optionally: Make the UI collapsible or move to below the panels to more faithfully match layout.
Consider removing excessive explanatory captions to declutter presentation.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The original figure has no sliders or interactive UI elements visible, only static vector fields.
Arrow density and placement in the generated figure differ slightly from the reference, especially at the panel edges.
Vector field magnitudes and normalization do not perfectly match the original, most visible in the left and right panels.
Labels for f are present and in correct font/placement, but may be slightly larger and lower than in the source.
Panel frames in generated figure are thicker and more prominent than in the original.
evaluation.notes: Excellent implementation with minor geometric/visual deviations; interactivity adds value over static figure.
action_items: Reduce panel frame thickness to better match the original.
Tune vector field normalization and lengths, especially at edges and corners.
Minimize arrow overlap at panel edges and corners for improved clarity.
Slightly adjust label position and size to better match the original figure annotation.
